title: ProtocolLab WebTransport Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe the executable WebTransport-over-HTTP/3 session, bidirectional-stream, and datagram contracts.
design_summary: Both lanes treat session establishment as a validity prerequisite. The stream lane measures one deterministic 65,536-byte client-initiated bidirectional-stream round trip; the datagram lane measures 32 deterministic 256-byte WebTransport datagram round trips on one session. Typed scenario objects own binding and payload semantics, and separate typed load profiles prevent stream and datagram evidence from sharing a comparison cohort. Package-backed executors fail closed on protocol substitution, byte-count or hash mismatch, datagram loss, unexpected failure, or timeout. Both smoke suites remain diagnostic and non-rankable until repeated, topology-controlled comparison policy exists.
key_components: typed WebTransport stream and datagram scenarios
data_and_state_considerations: Each measured operation opens a fresh authenticated session. The stream operation creates one client-initiated bidirectional stream and verifies one complete echoed payload. The datagram operation sends 32 index-derived payloads serially, verifies each complete echoed datagram and the ordered payload-set hash, then closes the session with code zero.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Ordinary HTTP/3 request-response success is not WebTransport evidence.
Session establishment without the selected stream or datagram data-path proof is insufficient.
Stream and datagram scenarios are separate comparison cohorts.
The draft protocol remains subject to upstream evolution, so exact package versions and observed protocol proof are retained.
Single-host smoke evidence is real but non-rankable.
alternatives_considered: A session-open-only result was rejected because it does not prove the data path.
Browser automation was rejected because a native protocol client provides more deterministic process and artifact control.
Combining stream and datagram work into one score was rejected because reliable streams and unreliable datagrams have different semantics and failure modes.
Multiple concurrent streams remain deferred until the two single-data-path contracts are live-proven.
risks: Independent libraries may implement different draft revisions.
A client library can hide protocol negotiation details unless exact response and connection evidence are retained.
Executor-side hashing can dominate very small samples, so the initial result is diagnostic rather than ranked.
